timeline_link.text: See on Timeline — Siege of Jerusalem

sections.header: Verses 1-13 -- Thrown In and Pulled Out

heb.paragraph: Jeremiah sinks into the mud (tit) at the bottom of the cistern (v.6). The physical detail -- "there was no water in it, only mud" -- makes the scene vivid: a dry cistern with accumulated sludge at the bottom, into which the prophet slowly sinks. The tit is both literal danger and metaphorical resonance -- the psalmist's cry "I sink in the miry depths" (Ps 69:2) becomes concrete reality.

refs.note: "He lifted me out of the slimy pit, out of the mud and mire" -- the psalm that Jeremiah's rescue fulfills.
Joseph thrown into a cistern and rescued through an outsider's intervention -- the typological parallel.
Another Ethiopian (the eunuch) receives divine favor -- the pattern of outsiders who act faithfully when insiders fail.

notes.note: "This man should be put to death. He is discouraging the soldiers." The charge is technically accurate: Jeremiah has been telling people to surrender. In wartime, this is treason. In prophetic terms, it is obedience.
"The king can do nothing to oppose you" -- Zedekiah's abdication of responsibility. The king hands the prophet to men who want him dead, washing his hands of the consequences.

Ebed-Melech, a Cushite (Ethiopian) court official, goes directly to the king and says: "These men have acted wickedly." He takes thirty men and rescues Jeremiah with old rags and worn-out clothes used as padding under the ropes to prevent injury.

The detail of the rags is remarkable: Ebed-Melech tells Jeremiah to "put these old rags and worn-out clothes under your arms to pad the ropes." The gentleness of the rescue contrasts with the brutality of the imprisonment.
Calvin celebrates Ebed-Melech: "A foreigner does what Israel's own leaders refuse to do. A Cushite shows more humanity than the covenant people. God uses the unlikely to shame the expected."
Ebed-Melech's name means "servant of the king." He may have been a eunuch (saris) serving in the royal court. His Cushite (Ethiopian/Sudanese) origin places him outside the covenant community.
The "storeroom beneath the treasury" where the rags are found suggests Ebed-Melech had access to palace storage -- he held a position of some authority.

Lundbom identifies Ebed-Melech as "the moral center of the narrative." While kings vacillate and officials plot murder, a foreign court servant acts with courage and compassion. His reward comes in 39:15-18.

Brueggemann reads the cistern as "the physical expression of prophetic marginalization." The one who speaks truth is literally pushed into a hole and covered over. But the truth cannot be buried; it rises from the mud.

hist.context: The officials demand Jeremiah's death for "weakening the hands" of the soldiers by telling people to surrender. Zedekiah capitulates: "He is in your hands. The king can do nothing to oppose you" (v.5). Jeremiah is lowered into the cistern of Malkijah. He would have died there except for Ebed-Melech, a Cushite official who appeals directly to the king and organizes a rescue with rags and ropes. Ebed-Melech -- a foreigner, possibly a eunuch -- becomes the hero of the chapter while Israel's own leaders try to murder their prophet.

heb.paragraph: Zedekiah confesses: "I am afraid (yare') of the Jews who have gone over to the Babylonians" (v.19). The king fears his own deserters more than God. This single confession reveals the spiritual bankruptcy of the monarchy: the king's decisions are driven by fear of human opinion rather than by the word of God.

refs.note: "Fear of man will prove to be a snare, but whoever trusts in the LORD is kept safe" -- the proverb that Zedekiah illustrates negatively.
Many leaders believed in Jesus but would not confess him "for they loved human praise more than praise from God" -- the same cowardice.

notes.note: Zedekiah summons Jeremiah to a private meeting at the "third entrance to the temple" and swears: "I will neither kill you nor hand you over." The secrecy reveals the king's powerlessness in his own court.

The final offer: "If you surrender to the officers of the king of Babylon, your life will be spared and this city will not be burned." The alternative: "this city will be handed over to the Babylonians and they will burn it down, and you yourself will not escape."
"I am afraid of the Jews who have gone over to the Babylonians." Zedekiah fears mockery and abuse from his own former subjects. Human opinion overrules divine command.
Calvin identifies Zedekiah's root sin: "Fear of man rather than fear of God. This is the universal temptation of the powerful: to care more about maintaining status than obeying truth."
The "women left in the royal palace" singing a taunt song adds a layer of shame: even the king's own household will mock him for trusting advisors who led him to ruin.

Lundbom reads this as "the last chance." Every previous consultation produced the same answer; this one adds the explicit emotional obstacle: fear. Zedekiah knows the truth, knows what to do, and chooses not to do it.

Brueggemann calls this "the most honest sentence Zedekiah ever speaks." The king drops all pretense and admits that his decisions are governed by fear, not faith. "The tragedy is not that he doesn't know the truth but that he does."

hist.context: The final private meeting between king and prophet is the most intimate scene in the book. Zedekiah swears an oath of protection, then asks for honest counsel. Jeremiah's answer is the same: surrender to Babylon and live; resist and the city burns. Zedekiah reveals his real obstacle: fear of his own people. The conversation ends with a cover story to protect both men from the officials. This is the last word between king and prophet.

lit.note: The cistern episode brings two characters into sharp focus: Ebed-Melech the courageous outsider and Zedekiah the paralyzed insider. The rescue and the final consultation are the last acts before the fall.

themes.note: Faith at 7 -- Ebed-Melech's courage contrasts Zedekiah's fear. Mercy at 6 reflects the rescue. Prophecy at 8: the unchanged word delivered even from a cistern.

The tit is both literal danger and metaphorical resonance -- the psalmist's cry "I sink in the miry depths" (Ps 69:2) becomes concrete reality.

Zedekiah confesses: "I am afraid (yare') of the Jews who have gone over to the Babylonians" (v.19). The king fears his own deserters more than God. This single confession reveals the spiritual bankruptcy of the monarchy: the king's decisions are driven by fear of human opinion rather than by the word of God.

trans.title: Comparing translations in Jeremiah in the Cistern

translations.text: And this is what the Lord says: ‘This city will certainly be given into the hands of the army of the king of Babylon, who will capture it.’ ”
Thus says the LORD: This city shall surely be given into the hand of the army of the king of Babylon and be taken.”

Then the officials said to the king, “This man should be put to death. He is discouraging the soldiers who are left in this city, as well as all the people, by the things he is saying to them. This man is not seeking the good of these people but their ruin.”

Then the officials said to the king, “Let this man be put to death, for he is weakening the hands of the soldiers who are left in this city, and the hands of all the people, by speaking such words to them. For this man is not seeking the welfare of this people, but their harm.”

debate: The "storeroom beneath the treasury" where the rags are found suggests Ebed-Melech had access to palace storage -- he held a position of some authorit…
Emphasises historical-critical, literary, or text-critical analysis.
Reads Jeremiah in the Cistern within the canonical framework of fulfilled prophecy and covenant theology.
Represented by Calvin, MacArthur, and the evangelical tradition.
Both readings engage the text seriously; they differ primarily in their hermeneutical starting points and assumptions about authorship and historical context.
